Our product roadmap covers three major initiatives this year. Initiative 1: AI-powered search We are integrating LLM-based search across all modules by Q2. The search will support natural language queries and return ranked results. Initiative 2: Mobile app redesign The mobile team is rebuilding the iOS and Android apps using React Native. Target launch is Q3 2025. Initiative 3: Enterprise SSO Single sign-on support for Okta, Azure AD, and Google Workspace. See the attached technical specification PDF for full details. Related pages: HR Policy Guide Technical Runbook Prometheus and Grafana – Beginner-Friendly Guide (DevOps) 1. What is Monitoring? (Before Prometheus) Simple meaning Monitoring means watching your systems continuously to make sure they are healthy. In DevOps: CPU usage Memory usage Disk space Application errors Response time 2. What is Prometheus? Simple definition Prometheus is a monitoring tool that collects and stores metrics as numbers over time. Think of Prometheus as: A data collector + database for system performance. What Prometheus does Collects metrics (CPU, memory, requests, errors) Stores them with timestamps Allows querying data Triggers alerts 3. What are Metrics? Simple meaning Metrics are numeric values that describe system behavior. • • • • • • • • • 1 Examples Metric Meaning CPU usage How busy CPU is Memory usage RAM used HTTP requests Number of requests Error count Failed requests Example metric: http_requests_total = 1200 Means: total HTTP requests so far. 4. Prometheus Architecture (Easy Explanation) Main components Prometheus Server – collects and stores data Targets – systems being monitored Exporters – expose metrics Alertmanager – sends alerts Grafana – visualization (external tool) 5. What is an Exporter? Simple definition An exporter is a program that converts system data into Prometheus format. Common exporters Node Exporter – Linux system metrics MySQL Exporter – Database metrics Blackbox Exporter – URL uptime Example Node Exporter exposes: CPU usage 1. 2. 3. 4. 5. • • • • 2 Memory usage Disk usage Prometheus pulls this data. 6. Pull vs Push (Important Concept) Prometheus uses PULL model Prometheus asks targets for metrics. Prometheus ---> Target (Give me metrics) Why pull is good Prometheus controls data collection Easier failure detection Push (used rarely) Used via Pushgateway for short-lived jobs. 7. Prometheus Installation (Basic Idea) Typical steps: Download Prometheus Configure prometheus.yml Start Prometheus server Access UI at localhost:9090 8. Prometheus Configuration File ( prometheus.yml ) Basic example global: scrape_interval: 15s scrape_configs: - job_name:

metrics. Prometheus ---> Target (Give me metrics) Why pull is good Prometheus controls data collection Easier failure detection Push (used rarely) Used via Pushgateway for short-lived jobs. 7. Prometheus Installation (Basic Idea) Typical steps: Download Prometheus Configure prometheus.yml Start Prometheus server Access UI at localhost:9090 8. Prometheus Configuration File ( prometheus.yml ) Basic example global: scrape_interval: 15s scrape_configs: - job_name: "node" • • • • 1. 2. 3. 4. 3 static_configs: - targets: ["localhost:9100"] Explanation Line Meaning scrape_interval How often data is collected job_name Name of monitored job targets Where metrics come from 9. Prometheus Web UI Access at: http://localhost:9090 You can: Run queries See targets Check alerts 10. PromQL (Prometheus Query Language) Simple meaning PromQL is used to ask questions about metrics. Example queries CPU usage node_cpu_seconds_total Rate (per second) rate(http_requests_total[5m]) • • • 4 Memory usage node_memory_MemAvailable_bytes 11. What is Grafana? Simple definition Grafana is a visualization tool. Think of Grafana as: A dashboard builder for metrics. Prometheus stores data → Grafana shows it beautifully. 12. Grafana Architecture Components Data source (Prometheus) Dashboards Panels Alerts 13. Installing Grafana (Concept) Steps: Install Grafana Access UI at localhost:3000 Login (admin/admin) 14. Adding Prometheus as Data Source in Grafana Steps: Settings → Data Sources Select Prometheus • • • • 1. 2. 3. 1. 2. 5 Save & Test 15. Grafana Dashboards What is a dashboard? A dashboard is a collection of charts. Example panels CPU usage graph Memory usage gauge Disk usage chart 16. Grafana Panels (Important) Each panel: Uses a PromQL query Displays data as graph/table/gauge Example query rate(node_cpu_seconds_total[5m]) 17. Alerts in Prometheus Simple meaning Alerts notify you when something goes wrong. Example CPU > 80% for 5 minutes Disk space < 10% 3. 4. • • • • • • • 6 18. Alertmanager What it does Receives alerts from Prometheus Sends notifications Alert channels Email Slack PagerDuty 19. Real-Life Monitoring Example Scenario You run a website. You monitor: CPU usage Memory HTTP errors If: CPU > 90% Errors increase ➡ Alert sent to Slack 20. Prometheus + Grafana Workflow Application → Exporter → Prometheus → Grafana ↓ Alertmanager 21. Common Beginner Mistakes Forgetting exporters Wrong target ports • • • • • • • • • • • • 7 Incorrect PromQL Not checking /metrics endpoint • • 8

A demo (short for demonstration) is a practical exhibition showing how a product, software, or system works, or a preliminary sample/recording. This is a flowchart: Click on the link below: https://dictionary.cambridge.org/dictionary/english/demo The image presents a flowchart designed to troubleshoot a malfunctioning lamp. The chart is structured as a series of questions and actions, guiding the user through a step-by-step process to identify and potentially resolve the issue. * **Initial Problem Statement** * The flowchart begins with a pink rectangle stating "Lamp doesn't work," indicating the initial problem. * **First Question: Lamp Plugged In?** * A yellow diamond-shaped box asks, "Lamp plugged in?" with two possible responses: "Yes" or "No." * If the answer is "No," the user is advised to "Plug in lamp" via a green rounded rectangle. * If the answer is "Yes," the flowchart proceeds to the next question. * **Second Question: Bulb Burned Out?** * Another yellow diamond-shaped box inquires, "Bulb burned out?" with two possible responses: "Yes" or "No." * If the answer is "Yes," the recommended action is to "Replace bulb" as indicated by a green rounded rectangle. * If the answer is "No," the user is instructed to "Repair lamp," also presented in a green rounded rectangle. In summary, the flowchart provides a straightforward and systematic approach to troubleshooting a non-functional lamp by checking if it is plugged in and if the bulb needs replacement or repair.

System Architecture: Our backend runs on AWS using ECS Fargate containers. The database is PostgreSQL 15 with read replicas in us-east-1 and eu-west-1. Deployment Process: Push code to main branch GitHub Actions triggers CI pipeline Docker image is built and pushed to ECR ECS service is updated via rolling deployment Incident Response: P1 incidents require response within 15 minutes. On-call rotation is managed via PagerDuty. Monitoring: Datadog for APM and infrastructure metrics Sentry for error tracking Grafana dashboards for business metrics External reference: https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/vector-database --- Attachments: none ---

Date: January 15, 2025 Attendees: Product, Engineering, Design Key decisions: Mobile app will use React Native (approved) AI search feature scoped to internal docs only for v1 Budget approved for 3 new engineering hires Action items: Engineering: finalize architecture doc by Jan 22 Product: update roadmap page with confirmed timelines Design: deliver mobile mockups by Feb 1 See full details: Product Roadmap 2025 --- Attachments: none ---

Leave Policy: All employees are entitled to 20 days of paid leave annually. Sick leave: 10 days per year, no carry forward. Parental leave: 16 weeks fully paid for primary caregiver. Code of Conduct: Employees must maintain professional behavior in all communications. Harassment of any kind is strictly prohibited. Expense Policy: Travel expenses must be submitted within 30 days of the trip. Meals are reimbursed up to $50 per day. All expenses above $500 require manager approval. The image presents a visually appealing infographic titled "9 Trending HR Policies," which showcases nine distinct HR policies. The title is prominently displayed in the center of the image within a white circle, accompanied by a multicolored border. **Title Section:** * The title "9 Trending HR Policies" is written in black text on a white background. * A multicolored border surrounds the title, featuring a gradient of colors including red, orange, yellow, green, blue, purple, and brown. **Policy Icons and Descriptions:** * The nine HR policies are represented by circular icons with a white background and a colored border, each accompanied by a brief description in black text. * The policies are arranged in a horizontal row, with the following titles and icons: * **Rewards & Recognition Policy:** A badge icon * **Business Travel Policy:** A briefcase icon * **Claim Reimbursement Policy:** A dollar sign icon * **Dress Code Policy:** A tie icon * **Leave Policy:** A calendar icon * **Performance Management Policy:** A target icon * **Salary Advance Policy:** A hand holding money icon * **Time & Attendance Policy:** A clock icon * **Timesheet Management Policy:** A timesheet icon **Background:** * The background of the image features a light blue color with various geometric shapes and lines in shades of blue, green, and yellow. Overall, the infographic provides a clear and concise overview of nine trending HR policies, making it an informative and engaging visual representation for HR professionals and individuals interested in human resources.

The screenshot is divided into several sections, each with its own distinct features and content. * **Left Sidebar** * The left sidebar contains a list of files and directories. * A dropdown menu is visible at the top of the sidebar. * The following files are listed: * confluence_history.db * Confluence_mcp_server.py * groq_agents.py * requirements.txt * Test_server.py * **Main Coding Area** * The main coding area displays a Python script with line numbers ranging from 81 to 168. * The script appears to be using the asyncio library and defines an asynchronous function called `run_agent`. * The code includes comments and docstrings, indicating that it is well-documented. * **Error Message** * A error message is displayed at the bottom of the screen. * The error message indicates that there is a problem with the `describe_image` tool. * The error message includes a stack trace, which suggests that the issue is related to a request to a server. * The error message also includes a suggestion for how to fix the issue. * **Terminal or Command Prompt** * A terminal or command prompt window is visible at the bottom of the screen. * The terminal displays a command prompt with a directory path and a Python executable. * The terminal also displays an error message related to the `groq_agents.py` file. In summary, the image shows a coding environment with a Python script that defines an asynchronous function called `run_agent`. The script appears to be well-documented, but there is an error message indicating that there is a problem with the `describe_image` tool. The error message includes a stack trace and a suggestion for how to fix the issue. The terminal or command prompt window displays a command prompt and an error message related to the `groq_agents.py` file.

Phone Introduction A phone is a telecommunications device that allows users to make and receive calls, send messages, and access various forms of media and information. History of Phones The first phone was invented in 1876 by Alexander Graham Bell, and since then, phones have undergone significant transformations, from rotary dial phones to touch-screen smartphones. Types of Phones Smartphones : These are advanced phones that can connect to the internet, run apps, and have a touch-screen interface. Feature Phones : These are basic phones that can make and receive calls, send texts, and have limited functionality. Landline Phones : These are phones that are connected to a physical phone line and are typically used in homes and offices. Phone Features Modern phones often come with a range of features, including: Camera : Allows users to take photos and videos. GPS : Enables location tracking and navigation. Bluetooth : Allows devices to connect to each other wirelessly. Wi-Fi : Enables internet connectivity. Phone Operating Systems There are several phone operating systems available, including: Android : An open-source operating system developed by Google. iOS : A proprietary operating system developed by Apple. Windows : A proprietary operating system developed by Microsoft. // Example of phone-related code console.log("Phone is ringing..."); Additional Information Some other key aspects of phones include their display size and resolution, battery life, and storage capacity. Conclusion In conclusion, phones have become an essential part of modern life, and their features and functionality continue to evolve with technological advancements. --- Attachments: none ---

The Model Content Protocol is a set of rules governing AI behavior. It ensures accurate and helpful responses. The protocol covers identity, query routing, and answering from content. It also includes guidelines for links, URLs, and content types. The goal is to provide concise and factual answers while maintaining context across conversations. Victoria Metrics Cluster Overview The Victoria Metrics Cluster is a high-availability setup for monitoring and storing metrics. The cluster consists of multiple components, including vmstorage , vminsert , vmselect , vmagent , nginx , alertmanager , and grafana . The primary technology stack includes Docker and Docker Compose. Architecture / How It Works The cluster architecture includes the following components: vmstorage (data layer): 2 instances, each with persistent volumes enabled vminsert (write layer): 2 instances, each forwarding data to both storage nodes vmselect (query layer): 2 instances, each connecting to both storage nodes nginx (load balancer): distributes query traffic across both vmselect nodes and provides automatic failover vmagent (scrape layer): 2 instances, each reading scrape config from scrape.yml and remote writing to both vminsert nodes alertmanager and grafana : used for alerting and visualization The data flow is as follows: node-exporters → vmagent1 & vmagent2 → vminsert1 & vminsert2 → vmstorage1 & vmstorage2 → vmselect1 & vmselect2 → nginx → grafana Setup & Installation To set up the cluster, ensure Docker and Docker Compose are installed and running. Then, follow the instructions in the docker-compose.yml file to start the containers. Configuration Configuration files include: docker-compose.yml : defines the services and their configurations nginx.conf : configures the nginx load balancer scrape.yml : defines the scrape jobs for vmagent targets.json : contains monitored endpoints and labels rules.yml : defines alerting rules alertmanager.yml : configures the alertmanager Usage To use the cluster, access the grafana dashboard at http://localhost:3000 . The data source type is Prometheus, and the URL is http://vmselect-lb:8481/select/0/prometheus . Key Files & Structure The project structure is as follows: vm-cluster/ │ ├── docker-compose.yml ├── nginx.conf ├── scrape.yml ├── targets.json ├── rules.yml ├── alertmanager.yml ├── data/ Dependencies The cluster uses the following main libraries and tools: victoriametrics/vmstorage victoriametrics/vminsert victoriametrics/vmselect victoriametrics/vmagent nginx prom/node-exporter grafana Contributing / Development Notes To contribute to the project or develop new features, follow these steps: Clone the repository Modify the configuration files as needed Run docker-compose up -d to start the containers Access the grafana dashboard to verify the changes --- Attachments: none ---
